First, the function to compute the LPS array:

vector<int> computeLPS(const string& pattern) {
    int m = pattern.size();
    vector<int> lps(m, 0);
    int length = 0; // length of the previous longest prefix suffix
    for (int i = 1; i < m; ++i) {
        while (length > 0 && pattern[i] != pattern[length]) {
            length = lps[length - 1];
        }
        if (pattern[i] == pattern[length]) {
            length++;
            lps[i] = length;
        } else {
            lps[i] = 0;
        }
    }
    return lps;
}

Then, the function to find all occurrences:

vector<int> findOccurrences(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
    vector<int> occurrences;
    int n = text.size();
    int m = pattern.size();
    if (m == 0 || n < m) return occurrences;

    vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
    int i = 0, j = 0;
    while (i < n) {
        if (text[i] == pattern[j]) {
            i++;
            j++;
            if (j == m) {
                occurrences.push_back(i - j);
                j = lps[j - 1];
            }
        } else {
            if (j != 0) {
                j = lps[j - 1];
            } else {
                i++;
            }
        }
    }
    return occurrences;
}

    return result;
}

Now, considering the time complexity.

Finding A and B using KMP is O(n + m), where n is the length of s, and m is the length of a or b.

#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> beautifulIndices(string s, string a, string b, int k) {
        vector<int> A = findOccurrences(s, a);
        vector<int> B = findOccurrences(s, b);

                result.push_back(i);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
    
private:
    static vector<int> computeLPS(const string& pattern) {
        int m = pattern.size();
        vector<int> lps(m, 0);
        int length = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i < m; ++i) {
            while (length > 0 && pattern[i] != pattern[length]) {

            } else {

        return lps;

    static vector<int> findOccurrences(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
        vector<int> occurrences;
        int n = text.size();
        int m = pattern.size();
        if (m == 0 || n < m) return occurrences;
        
        vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
        int i = 0, j = 0;

            } else {

                } else {
                    i++;
                }
            }
        }
        return occurrences;
    }
};
</think>

To solve this problem, we need to identify all beautiful indices in a given string `s`. An index `i` is considered beautiful if it meets two conditions: the substring starting at `i` matches string `a`, and there exists another index `j` such that the substring starting at `j` matches string `b` and the absolute difference between `i` and `j` is at most `k`.

### Approach
1. **Pattern Matching with KMP Algorithm**: We use the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm to efficiently find all starting indices where `a` and `b` occur in `s`. This helps in identifying potential candidates for beautiful indices.
2. **Binary Search for Efficient Range Check**: For each index `i` where `a` occurs, we use binary search to check if there exists an index `j` where `b` occurs within the range `[i - k, i + k]`. This ensures that we efficiently determine if the proximity condition is met.

### Solution Code
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <string>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> beautifulIndices(string s, string a, string b, int k) {
        vector<int> A = findOccurrences(s, a);
        vector<int> B = findOccurrences(s, b);
        
        vector<int> result;
        for (int i : A) {
            int left = i - k;
            int right = i + k;
            auto it = lower_bound(B.begin(), B.end(), left);
            if (it != B.end() && *it <= right) {
                result.push_back(i);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
    
private:
    static vector<int> computeLPS(const string& pattern) {
        int m = pattern.size();
        vector<int> lps(m, 0);
        int length = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i < m; ++i) {
            while (length > 0 && pattern[i] != pattern[length]) {
                length = lps[length - 1];
            }
            if (pattern[i] == pattern[length]) {
                length++;
                lps[i] = length;
            } else {
                lps[i] = 0;
            }
        }
        return lps;
    }
    
    static vector<int> findOccurrences(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
        vector<int> occurrences;
        int n = text.size();
        int m = pattern.size();
        if (m == 0 || n < m) return occurrences;
        
        vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while (i < n) {
            if (text[i] == pattern[j]) {
                i++;
                j++;
                if (j == m) {
                    occurrences.push_back(i - j);
                    j = lps[j - 1];
                }
            } else {
                if (j != 0) {
                    j = lps[j - 1];
                } else {
                    i++;
                }
            }
        }
        return occurrences;
    }
};
```

### Explanation
1. **KMP Algorithm**: The `computeLPS` function computes the Longest Prefix Suffix (LPS) array for the pattern, which is used to optimize pattern matching by avoiding unnecessary character comparisons. The `findOccurrences` function uses this LPS array to find all starting indices of the pattern in the text efficiently.
2. **Binary Search for Proximity Check**: For each index `i` where `a` occurs, we determine the range `[i - k, i + k]` and use binary search to check if there is any index `j` in the precomputed list of `b` occurrences within this range. This ensures that we efficiently check the proximity condition.

This approach ensures that we efficiently find all beautiful indices with a time complexity of O(n + m), where n is the length of `s` and m is the length of the patterns `a` and `b`. The use of binary search further optimizes the range checking step.
